details.text: Derivative: دُخَانٌ refers to smoke and is defined as that which rises from fire from the fragments which are yet to be completely burned away.
Derivative: صَاعِقَةً – It refers to an explosive sound that will destroy you. It can mean a thunderbolt and here it refers to a punishment like that which destroyed ‘Aad and Thamud.

Derivative: نَّحِسَاتٍ is the plural of نَحِس and it means unfortunate and disastrous. The use of the plural form اَتٍ in نَّحِسَاتٍ implies that the days it is describing were less than ten in number.
Derivative: صَرْصَرًا is a ferocious cold wind or a screeching wind. It comes from صِرّ which refers to extreme coldness. صَرْصَرًا is an exaggerated form of صِرّ and its cold is even more extreme.
Derivative: الْهُونِ from هَانَ which means to become lowly, contemptible, humiliated and degraded.
Derivative: يُوزَعُونَ from أَوْزَعَ which means to organise and arrange into units or rows where the last of them will be gathered with the last of them to be driven into the fire of hell.

Derivative: تَنزِيلٌ (a revelation) employs تَنْوِين (Tanween) which can be used in the Arabic language for the purpose of تَفْخِيم (Tafkheem – To inflate and magnify). Therefore, it can imply “a great revelation” i.e., something that is magnificent and has a great standing.
Derivative: أَرْدَا / أَرْدَى – Means to make fall of a cliff or high place. It implies to fall into ruin.

Derivative: يَسْتَعْتِبُوا from اِسْتَعْتَبَ. عَتَبَ means to reproach someone and tell them that they have done wrong. اِسْتَعْتَبَ is to attempt “to make amends, excuses or appeasement” for the wrong that has been done.
Derivative: قَيَّضْنَا from قَيَّضَ. It means to hatch (like a chicken), to prepare something or to assign something or someone to a task.

Derivative: قُرَنَاءَ is the plural of قَرِين from the verb قَرَنَ which means to be joined, connected, associated and linked. قُرَنَاءَ are those “devil associates” who are linked to people and beautify evil deeds for them.

Derivative: الْغَوْا from لَغَا – لَغْوٌ means to talk nonsense or about inappropriate or unrelated diversionary matters. In this context it means to interrupt and disturb the Prophet’s ﷺ recitation of the Qur’an with speech or shouting to divert from it.

Derivative: لَنُذِيقَنَّ from أَذاقَ which means to make taste. It has three extra layers of emphasis on it providing it with the strongest possible meaning. Firstly, with the لَام التَّوْكِيد (Laam At-Tawkid – Laam of emphasis), then with the نُون التَّوْكِيد (Nun At-Tawkid – Nun of emphasis) and finally with the Shaddah upon the Nun, providing it with even greater emphasis i.e., three levels of emphasis above the normal.

Derivative: قُرْآنًا – It is مَنْصُوب عَلَى المَدْح which means that it is preceded by a verb of praise and has consequently taken the accusative case (حالة النَّصْب) and is describes the greatness of the Qur’an.

Derivative: فُصِّلَتْ from فَصَّلَ – تَفْصِيل. It means to separate things one from the other and to make sure every part of it is understood. It reflects a process or methodology of breaking something apart and dealing with its parts one part at a time. Also, to present in detail.
Derivative: كِتَابٌ implies a “magnificent book” due to the use of the تَنْوِين (Tanween) which can be used in the Arabic language for the purpose of تَفْخِيم (Tafkheem – To inflate and magnify).
Derivative: تَشْتَهِي from اِشْتَهَى which means to strongly desire, wish for and crave.
Derivative: نُزُلًا refers to the introductory hospitality and the pre-prepared welcoming food served to a guest.

Derivative: يُلَقَّا / يُلَقَّى – Means to be made to meet which implies here to be granted something or to receive or attain it i.e., the attribute and standing of being أَحْسَن (Better) as mentioned in the verse two verses before this one (41:33).
Derivative: يَنزَغَ from نَزَغَ – نَزْغٌ – It means to stir, poke and prod. It implies stirring things up or in the case of Shaitan, it refers to his stirring and prodding to make you slip.
Derivative: يَسْأَمُونَ from سَئِمَ means to tire of something or to get fed up with it. Can imply irritation and being annoyed as the motivation for being tired of something or fed up with it.

Derivative: يُلْحِدُونَ from أَلْحَدَ originates from a grave that has crookedness in it. From that it means to deviate, distort or incline away from what is right and correct. It reflects an inclination against something and a bias. In this case it is against the verses and is manifested in attempts to distort their meanings.

- إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِالذِّكْرِ لَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ – “Verily those who have disbelieved in the Dhikr (i.e., Al-Qur’an) after it has come to them …” The answer to this statement is omitted and the omitted answer can be understood to refer to the terrible consequences that they will face.

- “To be called from a place that is far away” is an expression to mean that they cannot hear i.e., because of their disbelief and their deafness and blindness they are closed to receiving the guidance even though they may be directly in front of you. A similar meaning is that they are being called from a place of misguidance that is so far away from guidance.
Derivative: مُرِيبٍ implies putting others in doubt. Here it is the description of شَكٍّ (Shakk – Doubt) and implies that the doubt is infectious in the sense that it is a doubt that spreads doubt.
Derivative: أَكْمَامِ from كَمَّ which means to cover and it refers to the coverings of fruits and crops in their various forms i.e., sheathes, skins etc.

Derivative: مَّحِيصٍ – حَيْص is a difficulty or predicament that you cannot escape from. مَّحِيصٍ refers to a place that you manage to find to escape to of find shelter in when faced by a predicament.
Derivative: قَنُوطٌ means to be extremely pessimistic and despondent.
Derivative: يَئُوسٌ from يِئِسَ. يَئُوسٌ means to completely lose hope and to fall into abject despair.
Derivative: وَقْرٌ refers to a figurative blocking, a heaviness in the ears and deafness.
Derivative: أَكِنَّةٍ is the plural of كِنان and means a shelter or cover, implying to be blocked off.
Derivative: غَلِيظٍ refers to toughness, harshness and ruthlessness. From غِلْظَة something that has no flexibility in it.

Derivative: عَرِيضٍ from عَرَضَ which means to present something. عَرِيضٍ means to constantly be presenting something. Here, the Du’aa (supplication) which he engages in is described as عَرِيضٍ which means that is constant and continuous in it.
- نَأَىٰ بِجَانِبِهِ - It literally means to stay distant or aloof to his side” and it is a metaphor for acting in an arrogant manner.

Derivative: شِقَاقٍ means a schism, gap and dissension from شَقَّ which means to split. It implies stubbornness that prevents someone from agreeing with another person or over something, like there being a gap or gulf that cannot be bridged, or difference that can’t be reconciled.
Derivative: مِرْيَةٍ refers to a doubt that is not meant to exist; about something that is certain. اِمْتَرَى

Derivative: اسْتَقِيمُوا from اِسْتَقَامَ. قَامَ means to stand and اِسْتَقَامَ means to try one’s best to stand and to try hard to ensure that something remains straight and does not bend or waiver. It also means to commit oneself, to remain standing and firm, in order to hold one’s ground.
Derivative: مَمْنُونٍ – It means to be cut off. غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ means uninterrupted and not running out.
Derivative: أَندَادًا is the plural of نِدٌّ and means a rival or competitor.
